title: PCE Hits 3-Year High as Global Rate-Hike Signals Intensify
subhead: US inflation broke above 4% for the first time since 2023, keeping a Fed hike firmly on the table, while the BOJ and ECB simultaneously signaled further tightening — a rare synchronized hawkish shift as oil eases and geopolitical risks reshape the rate outlook.

sections.heading: US PCE Inflation Breaks Above 4%, Keeping Fed Hike in Play
sections.body:
The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge came in at 4.1% year-over-year for May, the highest reading in three years, according to Reuters. Core PCE — which strips out food and energy — rose 3.4%, the fastest since October 2023. The data sealed what had already been shifting market expectations: BofA and Deutsche Bank now forecast a 75bp rate hike as early as September, a dramatic reversal from their prior calls for steady rates, driven by persistent inflation and a more hawkish Fed under new Chair Kevin Warsh (Reuters).

Warsh, who took the helm earlier this year, has publicly defied President Trump's calls for rate cuts, making clear that the Fed's inflation fight takes priority. Nearly half of FOMC policymakers now project at least one rate hike before year-end (CryptoBriefing). Two-year Treasury yields rose 0.16% on June 17 alone following the Fed's last meeting, reflecting the bond market repricing for tighter policy. The Fed's current target range sits at 3.50-3.75%.

The inflation surge has structural roots beyond energy. Even as oil prices have collapsed back toward pre-war levels, services inflation and sticky wage growth continue to push core measures higher — suggesting the Fed's problem won't solve itself even if geopolitical risk premiums continue fading.
sections.whyItMatters: With PCE above 4%, the window for rate cuts has effectively closed. Markets are now pricing hikes, not cuts — a complete reversal from the start of the year that reshapes everything from mortgage rates to emerging-market capital flows.

sections.heading: Tokyo CPI Accelerates as BOJ Hawks Circle the Yen
sections.body:
Tokyo's key inflation gauge rose 1.6% year-over-year in June, the first acceleration in eight months, keeping the Bank of Japan on track for further rate hikes. The core-core measure excluding both fresh food and energy — closely watched by the BOJ — climbed 1.9%, while overall CPI rose 1.7% (Bloomberg/Singapore Business Times).

Hawkish BOJ board member Naoki Tamura called for rate hikes 'once every few months,' arguing the bank should bring its policy rate closer to 2%. Governor Kazuo Ueda reiterated his stance that inflation risks overshooting the 2% target. The BOJ delivers its next policy decision on July 31.

Meanwhile, the yen hovers near 40-year lows against the dollar, with USD/JPY trading around 161.75-161.93. Japan's Ministry of Finance has refrained from direct FX intervention despite repeated verbal warnings, creating what traders describe as a 'cat-and-mouse' dynamic (EconoTimes). Japan's government also released a draft economic blueprint urging the BOJ to support private demand — a signal that complicates the rate-hike trajectory by pressuring the bank to keep conditions accommodative even as inflation rises (Reuters).
sections.whyItMatters: The BOJ faces a triple bind: rising inflation demands hikes, a collapsing yen demands intervention, and the government wants accommodative policy. How it navigates July 31 will set the tone for Asian FX and carry trades globally.

sections.heading: Oil Retreats as Hormuz Traffic Recovers — But Iran Fires on Cargo Ship
sections.body:
Brent crude fell to $75.07/barrel and WTI to $71.79 on Friday morning in Asia, with both benchmarks heading for weekly losses of close to 7%. The retreat is driven by easing supply concerns: crude shipments through the Strait of Hormuz rose this week to their highest level since the US-Israeli conflict with Iran began in February, according to Reuters data cited by Singapore Business Times.

But the recovery is fragile. On Thursday, a cargo vessel was hit by a projectile near Oman — two US officials told Reuters that Iran fired on the ship as it attempted to transit the strait. The UN's shipping agency suspended its voluntary evacuation scheme. Iran warned that security of vessels outside designated Hormuz routes 'is not guaranteed.' IG analyst Tony Sycamore noted that markets are watching closely to see whether tanker traffic resumes or if 'these latest hurdles force producers to tap the brakes on planned production increases.'

JPMorgan has already cut its Brent forecast to $86 for Q3 and $80 for Q4, warning that weak demand and excess supply could force OPEC+ action. A Forbes analysis notes that while a supply glut looks probable for 2027, strategic stock replenishment by IEA members and China — which drew down roughly 800 million barrels of reserves during the conflict — could absorb a significant share of the surplus, delaying price pressure until later next year.

Separately, Venezuela's twin earthquakes (7.2 and 7.5 magnitude) killed at least 188 people and raised questions about the country's 1.2 million barrels/day of oil production, though preliminary assessments show limited damage to major infrastructure (AP, Singapore Business Times).
sections.whyItMatters: Oil's return to pre-war levels removes one inflation driver, but Hormuz remains a single-point-of-failure. Iran's strike on a cargo ship is a reminder that the ceasefire is fragile — any reversal sends oil and inflation expectations right back up.

sections.heading: ECB's Schnabel Calls for Further Rate Hikes as Energy Fades
sections.body:
ECB executive board member Isabel Schnabel said Thursday that the ECB 'will need to raise interest rates further' to bring inflation back to the 2% target, even as oil prices retreat. Reuters reports Schnabel pointed to persistent underlying price pressures despite the energy cooldown (MSN/Reuters).

The timing is striking: the same day, Bloomberg reported that ECB rate-hike forecasts were being pared back as oil regained pre-war levels, with some economists scaling back their tightening expectations. The divergence between Schnabel's hawkish rhetoric and market recalibration captures the ECB's dilemma — core inflation remains elevated even as headline pressures ease.

The G7 summit in Évian produced a joint recognition of macroeconomic imbalances, with leaders stating it is 'in everyone's interest to cooperate and coordinate economic policies,' and assigning the IMF a central role in monitoring (Élysée). The statement was notably cautious — no specific commitments on exchange rates or policy coordination, reflecting the deep disagreements between the US rate-hike path and Europe's more fragile recovery.
sections.whyItMatters: Schnabel's hawkish stance clashes with falling energy prices and a slowing European economy. If the ECB hikes while growth stalls, it deepens the divergence between US and European monetary policy — with implications for the euro, sovereign debt spreads, and cross-Atlantic capital flows.

sections.heading: Trump vs. Macron: Digital Tax Sparks 100% Tariff Threat at G7
sections.body:
Ahead of the G7 summit in Évian, President Trump threatened 100% tariffs on French wine and champagne unless France drops its 3% digital services tax on US technology firms. Trump told the New York Post he delivered the warning directly to President Macron (Financial Express, Fox Business).

Macron pushed back forcefully on French television: 'This digital tax, decided by Europeans, implemented by several countries, is part of our law. It's not the United States that decides on the Europeans' law.' France has collected hundreds of millions of euros annually from the levy, which targets large digital platforms operating across borders (Financial Express).

The standoff has wider implications: Canada dropped its own digital tax plans under US pressure, while the UK has kept its system in place. The dispute now feeds into broader G7 tensions over trade, with the Évian summit producing language about avoiding 'trade wars and financial crises' but no resolution on the digital tax question (Élysée).
sections.whyItMatters: This isn't just about wine — it's about whether the US can unilaterally veto European tax policy through tariff threats. If France holds firm, other countries with digital taxes (UK, Italy) face similar pressure, fracturing the G7 on a fundamental sovereignty question.

sections.heading: Iran Deal's Financial Architecture: Frozen Assets Won't Touch Tehran
sections.body:
The Trump administration is insisting that billions in frozen Iranian assets will never actually reach Tehran, according to a US official who spoke to the New York Post. The claim is central to selling the US-Iran memorandum of understanding domestically: economic relief for Iran without direct cash transfers to the Iranian government.

But the details remain contentious. Analyst Steve Rattner noted that the deal's figure is 'colossally higher' than the Obama-era Iran deal, particularly when including a proposed $300 billion in unfrozen assets and concessions (MSN/MSNBC). Meanwhile, Iran has seen a speedy rebound in oil exports after the deal granted immediate waivers on US sanctions — a tangible economic lifeline that Gulf allies view with deep unease (MSN).

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, touring Gulf states, rejected any Iranian tolls on Strait of Hormuz shipping and stressed the US won't do a deal 'at any price' (Yahoo News/Reuters). Arab Gulf states, meanwhile, fear the agreement could leave Tehran stronger while undermining Washington's credibility as a regional security guarantor (Moneycontrol). The UN suspended its Hormuz evacuation scheme after Iran struck a cargo ship on Thursday, underscoring how fragile the arrangement remains.
sections.whyItMatters: The financial architecture of the Iran deal — how frozen assets move, who controls them, whether sanctions waivers become permanent — will shape oil markets, Gulf security arrangements, and the credibility of US sanctions policy for years.

sections.heading: Gold Steadies Near $4,000 as Central Banks Buy at Record Pace
sections.body:
Gold is trading near $4,000/oz — down almost 8% in 2026 — yet central banks continue buying at a historic pace. Net official-sector gold demand hit 244 tonnes in Q1 2026, a 17% increase quarter-over-quarter, according to the World Gold Council survey cited by CryptoBriefing. Poland led with 31 tonnes, followed by Uzbekistan at 25 tonnes. China added 8 tonnes in April alone, extending an 18-month buying streak.

The BRICS+ bloc accounted for over 50% of all global central bank gold purchases between 2020 and 2024, with their collective share of world gold reserves rising from 11.2% to 17.4%. Some 45% of surveyed central banks plan to increase gold holdings in the coming year, rising to 53% among emerging and developing economies (World Gold Council/CryptoBriefing).

The buying spree has structural motivations: when the US froze Russian central bank assets in 2022, it demonstrated that dollar reserves can be weaponized. The dedollarization signal is clear, even as gold's price pullback reflects reduced geopolitical risk premiums and a stronger dollar.
sections.whyItMatters: Central bank gold demand creates a structural floor under prices even as tactical investors pull back. The 1,000+ tonnes/year pace of sovereign buying is a long-term bet against the dollar-based reserve system — one that doesn't reverse when oil prices fall.

bottomLine: Three central banks — the Fed, BOJ, and ECB — are simultaneously signaling tighter policy even as oil prices collapse back toward pre-war levels. The US PCE hitting 4.1% with BofA and Deutsche Bank now calling for a September hike is the clearest signal yet that the inflation story has shifted from 'transitory energy shock' to something stickier. In Japan, the BOJ faces a policy trilemma: rising inflation, a yen at 40-year lows, and a government that wants accommodative conditions. In Europe, Schnabel's hawkish call clashes with receding energy prices and a fragile recovery. Meanwhile, the US-Iran deal is reshaping oil markets — Brent at $75, Hormuz traffic recovering — but Iran's strike on a cargo vessel is a reminder that the ceasefire can unravel fast. The Trump-Macron digital tax standoff and the G7's inability to resolve it reveals deepening fractures in the alliance over sovereignty, taxation, and trade. Underneath it all, central banks are voting with their reserves: 244 tonnes of gold in Q1 alone, a structural shift away from the dollar that no single day's news will reverse.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, Singapore Business Times, AP, CryptoBriefing, World Gold Council, MSN, Financial Express, Fox Business, EconoTimes, Forbes, Morningstar, Élysée, EU Council, Moneycontrol, Yahoo News, Daily Business Group, IG
